coredata.dc:title: A rapid microwave synthesis of nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon nanodots as highly sensitive and selective fluorescence probes for ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Junxia
Yu, Jie
Feng, Suling
Su, Li
coredata.dc:description: A ultrafast one-step microwave-assisted method was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs) by using ethylenediamine as the carbon source and sulfamic acid as the surface passivation reagent. The morphology and the properties of N,S-CDs were explored by a series of techniques, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The prepared N,S-CDs exhibit bright blue photoluminescence with a high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) up to 28%, and high stability and excellent water solubility. A N,S-CDs-based fluorescent probe was developed for sensitive detection ascorbic acid (AA) in the presence of Cu2+, based on the mechanism that AA reduces Cu2+ to Cu+, then Cu+ quenches the fluorescence of N,S-CDs through electron or energy transfer due to the interaction between Cu+ and thiol ligand on the N,S-CDs surface. The observed linear response concentration range was from 0.057 to 4.0μM to AA with a detection limit as low as 18nM. The probe exhibited a highly selective response toward AA even in the presence of possible interfering substances, such as uric acid and citric acid. Moreover, these promising features made the sensing system used for the analysis of human serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave
Nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the conditions for preparing N,S-CDs
Optimization of the ascorbic acid detection conditions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology ln University of Henan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology ln University of Henan Province ( 15IRTSTHN 003 ) Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (inset, high-resolution HR-TEM image) and (B) size distribution of N,S-CDs.
FTIR spectrum of (A) N,S-CDs and (B) S-CDs, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra of ethylenediamine, sulfamic acid, H2SO4, N,S-CDs and S-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs and S-CDs. Inset: photographs of N,S-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right). The concentrations of N,S-CDs and S-CDs are 0.41mg/mL and 2.41mg/mL, respectively.
Excitation spectra of (A) N,S-CDs and (B) S-CDs with varying emission wavelength from 380nm to 510nm. The concentrations of N,S-CDs and S-CDs are 0.41mg/mL and 2.41mg/mL, respectively.
Fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs and S-CDs measured by monitoring the emission at 424 and 445nm when excited at 405nm, respectively.

(A) The F/F 0 change of N,S-CNs for different metal ions with the same concentration of 100μM. (B–D) The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system observed at 424nm in the presence of potentially interfering substances. The concentration of potentially interfering substances was 0.5μg/mL, and the concentration of AA was 2.84μM.
(A) Fluorescence and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of system. c(Cu2+)=0.275mM, Cu+ correspond to the saturated solution of CuCl, c(AA)=1.1μM, c(N,S-CDs)=0.0459mg/mL, HOAc–NaOAc=0.6ml (pH=5.5).

Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs–Cu2+ as function of AA concentration (0.057, 0.11, 0.17, 0.23, 0.34, 0.45, 0.57, 1.14, 1.70, 2.27, 2.84, 3.41, and 3.97µM) in aqueous solution. Reduced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) against the AA over the linear range of 0.057–3.97µM (inset).
Response mechanism of N,S-CDs to AA in the presence of Cu2+.
Results for the determination of AA in urine and serum samples.

head.ce:title: A rapid microwave synthesis of nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon nanodots as highly sensitive and selective fluorescence probes for ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Xinxiang Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ultrafast one-step microwave-assisted method was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs) by using ethylenediamine as the carbon source and sulfamic acid as the surface passivation reagent. The morphology and the properties of N,S-CDs were explored by a series of techniques, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The prepared N,S-CDs exhibit bright blue photoluminescence with a high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) up to 28%, and high stability and excellent water solubility. A N,S-CDs-based fluorescent probe was developed for sensitive detection ascorbic acid (AA) in the presence of Cu2+, based on the mechanism that AA reduces Cu2+ to Cu+, then Cu+ quenches the fluorescence of N,S-CDs through electron or energy transfer due to the interaction between Cu+ and thiol ligand on the N,S-CDs surface. The observed linear response concentration range was from 0.057 to 4.0μM to AA with a detection limit as low as 18nM. The probe exhibited a highly selective response toward AA even in the presence of possible interfering substances, such as uric acid and citric acid. Moreover, these promising features made the sensing system used for the analysis of human serum and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous interest by virtue of their distinctive superiority including outstanding water solubility, high photostability against blinking and photobleaching, tunable excitation and emission spectra, robust chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [1–4]. And they are very promising in numerous possible applications such as photocatalysis [5], optoelectronic devices [6], bioimaging [7], and optical sensor [1,2,4,8]. To date, a variety of methods have been proposed for preparing CDs, including arc-discharge [9], electrochemical oxidation [10], laser ablation [11], hydrothermal approach [7,12], ultrasonics [13] and microwave pyrolysis approach [14], etc. Among them, microwave pyrolysis has many advantageous features such as high efficiency in heating process, rapid carbonization reactions and simplicity of the instrumentation [15]. Therefore, microwave pyrolysis is a simple, fast and green method for synthesis of CDs with much lower cost.

As one of CDs with heteroatoms, nitrogen–sulfur co-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs) exhibit their fascinating performances in fluorescence, and most of the reported methods for the preparation of N,S-CDs were hydrothermal treatment. For example, Li et al. prepared N,S-CDs with high FLQY up to 73% and excitation-independent emission [3]. Xiong et al. synthesized highly photoluminescence N,S-CDs as fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ and cell imaging [7]. Wang et al. fabricated N,S-CDs for highly sensitive and selective detection of methotrexate in human serum [2]. However, the hydrothermal method suffers to some degree from shortcomings such as time-consuming processes, expensive equipment required, and high temperature, which hinder their wide applications. As a “green”, facile and feasible approach, microwave-assisted pyrolysis for N,S-CDs have been barely reported. Choi et al. prepared N,S-CDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of rice and N-acetyl-l-cysteine, and the FLQY is only 2.36% [14]. You et al. reported a microwave synthesis of N,S-CDs for bio-imaging of Hg2+ in living cells [16]. In addition, although a variety of CDs have been synthesized, it is still desired to diversify the surface group of CDs for enlarging their application in various fields [17]. Thus, to explore new microwave routes for synthesis of highly fluorescent, surface-different N,S-CDs is still a tricky challenge.

Ascorbic acid (AA) as an important anti-oxidant and vitamin, plays an important role in many biochemical processes such as free radical scavenging, cell development and immunity improvement [18,19]. The abnormal concentration levels of ascorbic acid in biological fluids are considered to be associated with some diseases such as cancer and diabetes mellitus [20] etc. Up until now, various methods have been employed for the detection of AA in biological samples, including electrochemistry [21,22], spectrophotometry [23], chemiluminescence [24], HPLC [25] and so on. Among these techniques, electrochemical methods commonly reported for AA, suffered from drawbacks such as high background, rather poor selectivity, weak reproducibility, and complex preparation of electrodes. Spectrophotometric methods showed limited linear range or relatively low sensitivity. Chemiluminescence methods used heavy metal-containing reagent. Chromatographic techniques required complicated sample preparation, and sophisticated and expensive instruments. In recent years, some available fluorescent probes have been employed for the quantitative determination of AA in biological samples [26–33] because of the advantages of high sensitivity, operational simplicity and rapid response. Yan and co-workers reported a CdTe QDs fluorescent probe with KMnO4 as the quencher for the detection of AA in human urine and plasma [26]. Yao and co-workers developed photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles for determination of AA in human urine, human blood serum, and human blood plasma samples, based on the synergistic action of the inner filter effect and the static quenching effect [29]. Tang and co-workers demonstrated cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH)-modified luminescence nanoparticles (Sr2MgSi2O7:1% Eu, 2% Dy) for detection and imaging of AA in living cells and in vivo [31]. Shao's group synthesized a reversible fluorescent probe by incorporating 4-amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidin-1-ol group into a 7-nitrobenzoxadiazole fluorophore for the cyclic detection of ClO−/AA in living HeLa cells [32]. However, most of these methods suffer from shortcomings such as usage of toxic heavy metal elements and hazardous reagent, complicated fabrication procedures of the probes, and disturbance of some reductive species including glutathione and cysteine. These shortcomings limited their applications in practical process. Therefore, there is a demand for sensitive, reliable, fast, and inexpensive detection of AA in biological fluids.

In the present work, we developed a simple synthesis of N,S-CDs via a one-step microwave-assisted route using ethylenediamine as the carbon source and sulfamic acid as N,S-doped precursors and the surface passivation reagent. The synthesis process can be completed within 2min. The as-prepared N,S-CDs displayed a relatively high fluorescent quantum yield of 28%. In addition, the N,S-CDs show a highly selective response to AA in the presence of Cu2+. Moreover, the N,S-CDs/Cu2+ could be used as a fluorescent probe for detection of ascorbic acid in urine and serum samples, which demonstrates its practical application in biological fluids. In comparison with other fluorescence probes employed for AA detection in urine and serum samples [26,28–30], the developed probe system showed high sensitivity.

Sulfamic acid was purchased from Beijing northwest Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). AA was obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Ethylenediamine was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). HOAc and NaOAc were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). The solution of copper(II) ion (2.5mM) was prepared from CuSO4·5H2O (Shanghai Zhenxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China)). The standard solution of AA (56.8μM) was prepared and kept in a refrigerator at 4°C. A HOAc–NaOAc buffer solution was used to maintain the pH of solutions at 5.5. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were carried out using a HITACHI H-8100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured with a Nicolet FTS NEXUS FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet instrument Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed by Thermo ESCALAB 250 (Thermo Electron Co., USA). The absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-1700 UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Co., Japan). All fluorescence spectra were performed using a FP-6500 spectrofluorophotometer (JASCO Corp. Japan). The absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured by a FLS-980 fluorescent spectrophotometer employing an integrating sphere (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The N,S-CDs were centrifuged using a TGL-16G centrifuge (Shanghai anting scientific instrument factory, China). The pH adjustments were monitored using a pHS-3C digital acidity meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd, China).

We developed a green, and simple method for synthesis of N,S-CDs by microwave-heating sulfamic acid and ethanediamine together. In a typical synthesis, 0.25mL of ethanediamine and 0.5g of sulfamic acid were dissolved into 10ml distilled water to obtain a transparent colorless solution. The mixture was heated in a domestic microwave oven (800W) for 2min, then cooled to room temperature naturally. The resulting dark brown solid was dispersed in 10ml distilled water. The obtained suspension was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min and filtered through a 0.10μm membrane. The as-prepared N,S-CDs solution was further diluted to 100mL with distilled water to obtain a dilute carbon dots solution at a concentration of 4.08mg/mL. In the control experiments, 0.25mL of ethanediamine and 0.27ml of the concentrated H2SO4 were placed in 10ml distilled water and heated in a domestic microwave oven (800W) for 2min for preparation of sulfur- doped carbon nanodots (S-CDs). Other procedures were the same as those for N,S-CDs.

0.9mL of 0.51mg/mL N,S-CDs, 1.1ml of 2.5mM Cu2+ and 0.6mL of the pH=5.5 HAc–NaAc buffer solution were added to a series of 10mL volumetric flask, followed by the addition of different concentrations of the standard solution of ascorbic acid or sample solution. They were diluted to the scale with distilled water and mixed thoroughly. Finally, the solution was transferred to a fluorescence cuvette. In order to obtain favorable fluorescence spectra, the excitation and emission slit was set to 5nm in the case of excitation wavelength at 391nm. Fluorescence intensity (F) of the solution was recorded under the emission wavelength at 424nm (the reagent blank was marked as F 0). F 0–F is the decreased fluorescence intensity and marked as ΔF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions for preparing N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As we have known, ethylenediamine has been widely served as surface passivation for the preparation of various carbon dots [2,34,35]. In this work, we employed ethylenediamine as the carbon source and sulfamic acid as N,S-doped precursors and the surface passivation reagent to synthesize N,S-CDs successfully. In order to obtain highly luminescent N,S-CDs, we optimized the synthetic conditions towards 0.5g sulfamic acid dissolved in 10ml of distilled water in 800W microwave oven. Firstly, we tested the dependence of fluorescence of N,S-CDs on the volume of ethylenediamine in the range of 0.15–0.40mL. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs reached the highest value when ethylenediamine was 0.25mL. The varying ethylenediamine from 0.25 to 0.40mL cause a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. The reason may be that the interaction of –NH2 of excessive ethylenediamine with carboxyl groups on the surface of N,S-CDs induced the aggregation of N,S-CDs, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs increased with increasing reaction time up to 120s (Fig. S1B). As a result, it was chosen as the optimum condition for preparing N,S-CDs that 0.5g sulfamic acid in 10ml of distilled water containing 0.25mL ethylenediamine was heated for 120s in 800W microwave oven.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is commonly employed as a powerful tool to identify the morphology and size of CDs. Fig. 1A represents the typical TEM image of the as-prepared N,S-CDs, revealing that the N,S-CDs were spherical and well monodispersed with an average diameter of 6.3nm (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM image clearly displays that the N,S-CDs had well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interplanar spacing of 0.32nm (Fig. 1A, inset) which corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [7], indicating that the N,S-CDs produced graphitization to some extent during reactions. S-CDs show similar morphology to N,S-CDs (Fig. S2).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was used to identify the surface functional groups of N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2, the peaks at 3450, 3165, 2520 and 1035cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, S–H and –S
O
3
−
, respectively [14,17,36]. These results demonstrate that there are abundant amino groups and some hydroxyl, thiol ligand and sulfite on the surface of the as-synthesized S,N-CDs, which make the N,S-CDs hydrophilic and stable in an aqueous system. Additionally, the band within the range of 3054–2913cm−1 is attributed to C–H stretching vibrations, and six peaks at 1286, 1681, 1618, 1437, 1162 and 1520cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of C=S, C=O, C=C, C–N, C–O and bending vibration of CON–H, respectively [2,7,14,17]. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded to explore the components and structures of the resultant carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S3A, four peaks at 167.5, 284.7, 399.5 and 531.6eV originate from S 2p (0.08%), C 1s (72.76%), N 1s (6.92%) and O 1s (20.24%), respectively, suggesting that sulfur, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements emerge on the surface of N,S-CDs. The measured signal of S 2p is much weaker than that of others, indicating a lower content of sulphur in the N,S-CD. The C 1s spectrum can be divided into five component peaks at 288.4, 287.5, 285.9, 285.3 and 284.6eV, which are consistent with the C=O/C=N, C–O, C–N, C–S and C–C/C=C bonds, respectively [4,7]. The exhibit of C=C and C–N enhances the fluorescence efficiency significantly [7]. The deconvoluted N 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 400.9 and 399.4eV, corresponding to graphitic N–(C)3 [37] and pyridinic C–N–C [7], respectively, which confirms the newly formed polyaromatic structures containing C–N and C=N [7]. The two fitted peaks at 533.3 and 531.6eV in O 1s spectrum may be attributed to C–O and C=O groups, respectively [2,12]. The S 2p spectrum can be resolved into four peaks at 170.8, 168.1, 165.9 and 164.2eV, which confirms sulfur in four groups of –C–SO4,–C–S
O
3
−
, –C–S–(S 2p1/2) and –C–S–(S 2p3/2), respectively [2,7]. Thus, both FT-IR spectrum and XPS confirm the incorporation of nitrogen and sulphur into the structure of N,S-CD.

The optical properties of CDs were explored by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 3, both N,S-CDs and S-CDs exhibit similar UV–vis spectra with both shoulders at 255nm and absorption bands centered at 330nm. Meanwhile, only N,S-CDs have two slight shoulders at 374 and 390nm. The peak at 255nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, producing nearly no observed photoluminescence signal [3,38]. The band at 330nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition of C=O [38]. The N,S-CDs and S-CDs emit strong blue luminescence when irradiated by UV light (365nm). Up to date, several fluorescence emission mechanisms have been proposed, such as quantum confinement effect [39] and surface defects [40]. Because of the narrow size distribution of the two samples (see below), surface state emission should play a key role in their photoluminescence. The N,S-CDs absorbance shoulders at 374 and 390nm correspond to the excitation peaks at 374 and 391nm, respectively (Fig. 3). Therefore, the two shoulders above should be ascribed to the trapping of excited state energy of the N,S-CDs surface states, leading to strong fluorescence emission [3,11]. As shown in Fig. 4, the excitation wavelength changes with varying emission wavelength, suggesting the different excited state energy on the surface of N,S-CDs and S-CDs. The excitation spectra of the N,S-CDs were very different from those of the S-CDs when emission wavelength changed from 400 to 440nm. These spectral differences above suggested that the introduction of nitrogen-doping forms a new kind of surface state. Fig. S4 exhibits the PL spectra of the as-synthesized N,S-CDs under different concentrations. The fluorescence emission peak at 442nm red shifted to 506nm and decreased remarkably as the concentration of N,S-CDs increased from 0.82mg/mL to 8.16mg/mL. The result revealed that concentration can alter the distribution of the emission wavelength of N,S-CDs, i.e. N,S-CDs show concentration-dependent emission, which is assigned to surface states change in N,S-CDs, resulting from the enhanced particle aggregation in concentrated solutions. The two samples also exhibit the excitation-dependent feature. The emission wavelength red shifted from 426 to 516nm for N,S-CDs and from 427 to 520nm for S-CDs with increasing excitation wavelength from 360 to 470nm (Fig. S5A). It has been reported that the excitation wavelength dependence of CDs reflects effect from particles of different sizes in sample and a distribution of different surface states [3,40] or another mechanism altogether [40,41]. The full width at a half-maximum at the different excitation wavelengths were 83±10nm and 88±6nm for the N,S-CDs and S-CDs, respectively, confirming a narrow size distribution of the two samples, which are in accordance with the results of the TEM (Figs. 1 and S2). Therefore, the excitation-dependent PL behavior is attributed to the different emissive sites traps with different energy levels resulting from the existence of different functional groups on the CDs [42]. Moreover, there was a good linear relationship between the excitation and emission wavelength with a linear equation of λ em=23.4λ ex+46.3 (λex≧391nm, r=0.977) for the N,S-CDs and λ em=18.2λ ex+126.3(λ ex≧360nm, r=0.986) for the S-CDs. Thus, the excitation wavelength could be estimated to obtain specific color such as purple, blue and green, according to the equation. The N,S-CDs and S-CDs showed maximum emission at 424 and 445nm, respectively, when excited with 391nm UV light. The 21nm blue-shift of the PL emission may be attributed to the strong electron affinity of N atoms doped in the N,S-CDs [43]. The absolute quantum yield of the prepared N,S-CDs was calculated to be 28%, which is comparable to that of N,S-CDs produced by microwave-assisted synthesis methods [14,16], whereas the FLQY of S-CDs was measured to be 13%. The high luminescence of the N,S-CDs could be possibly ascribed to the existence of more C–N bands (Fig. 2), which is in accord with the result of the reference [7], and the N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the CDs [4,44]. The fluorescence decay of the two samples showed three exponential function, confirming that the surface states are not uniform ( Fig. 5). The lifetime of the N,S-CDs was 3.94ns, which is slightly longer than that of the S-CDs (3.73ns). These results above suggested that the fluorescent center of the N,S-CDs is not the same as that of the S-CDs.

The fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs were measured in the presence of a series of metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6A, no obvious fluorescence signal changes were observed with the addition of 16 metal ions including Li+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cr(VI) at the concentration of 100μM, respectively. This confirms the high stability of the photoluminescence of N,S-CDs in saline water and their chemical inertness. A weak quenching effect was observed with addition of Ag+ and Hg2+. This may be attributed to the weak affinity of N,S-CDs to them. Interestingly, we found that the addition of AA made the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs reduce sharply in the presence of Cu2+ ( Fig. 7A). Because AA, a strong reductant, can reduce Cu2+ to Cu+, the luminescence quenching was considered to stem from interaction of N,S-CDs with Cu+. To identify the interaction, UV–vis spectroscopy was obtained, as displayed in Fig. 7B. Compared to N,S-CDs and Cu+ alone, the mixture of N,S-CDs with Cu+ produced a new peak at 240nm. This result indicated that the interaction occurs, which may be attributed to the affinity of thiol ligand on the surface of N,S-CDs to Cu+. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs–Cu2+–AA could be result from that the affinity of the thiol ligand of N,S-CDs to Cu+ produced by redox reaction of AA and Cu2+ facilitates electron or energy transfer, leading to a decrease of the fluorescence of N,S-CDs, as shown in Scheme 1. Based on the mechanism, N,S-CDs with Cu2+ were developed as sensors to respond AA. In addition, the S-CDs/Cu2+ also showed similar response to AA, but low sensitivity. The higher sensitivity of the N,S-CDs/Cu2+ toward AA could be attributed to the high affinity binding between thiol ligand of N,S-CDs and Cu+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ascorbic acid detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect AA sensitively, experimental conditions including buffers, pH and the concentration of Cu2+ and N,S-CDs were optimized. As we have known, buffers have effect on the sensitivity of analytical method. Therefore, we tested the effect of nine buffer solutions including Na2HPO4–KH2PO4, NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4, Na2HPO4–citric acid, NaOH–KH2PO4, sodium borate–KH2PO4, NaOH–potassium hydrogen phthalate, Tris–HCl, Britton–Robinson and HOAc–NaOAc. The results showed that HOAc–NaOAc buffer solution was preferable for improving the sensitivity. Subsequently, the pH effect was investigated by using HOAc–NaOAc buffer solution in the range of pH 3.5–6.2. At lower pH, AA is easily oxidized; at higher pH, Cu2+ is hydrolyzed, which results in producing less Cu+. Thus, the quenched fluorescence signal reached the highest value at pH=5.5 (Fig. S6A). Furthermore, the dosage of HOAc–NaOAc buffer was also studied. The result revealed that ΔF reached the maximum when HOAc–NaOAc buffer was 0.6mL (Fig. S6B). In addition, the effect of Cu2+ concentration on quenched fluorescence signal was tested by monitoring the dependence of ΔF on the Cu2+ concentration in the range from 0.1 to 0.4mM. As shown in Fig. S6C, ΔF obtained the highest value when the concentration of Cu2+ was 0.275mM. Likewise, the effect of N,S-CDs concentration in the range of 0.0102–0.0714mg/mL was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6D, ΔF was maximum when the concentration of N,S-CDs was increased to 0.0459mg/mL. Considering the sensitivity of the method, 0.6mL of pH 5.5 HAc–NaAc buffer, 0.275mM of Cu2+ and 0.0459mg/mL of N,S-CDs were used as the optimal conditions for further study.

Under the optimized conditions, the AA-induced fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs/Cu2+ was measured. The quenched fluorescence intensity was closely related to the concentration of AA in the range from 0.057 to 4.0μM ( Fig. 8). The linear fitting equation for the quantification of AA was ΔF=−2.17+107 c (n=11) with a correlation coefficient R 2=0.9998. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.018μM (3s), which is comparable to that of other fluorescence probes employed for AA detection in urine and serum samples [26,28–30]. For 11 parallel determinations of 1.1µM AA, the relative standard deviation was obtained to be 1.2%, which indicated that the proposed method has excellent reproducibility for detecting AA.

Besides sensitivity requirement, high selectivity is of importance for probes to assay real samples. Thus, we further examined the PL intensity change of the N,S-CDs/Cu2+ in the presence of representative anions and organic molecules under the same conditions, including uric acid, citric acid, glutathione, cysteine, S2
O
3
2 −
, and S
O
3
2 −
etc. In comparison with the species studied, only AA is able to quench the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs/Cu2+ (Fig. 6B–D). In addition, selective detection of AA was also estimated by examining the tolerance levels of potentially interfering substances with an error of no more than 5%, as shown in Table 1. All the results above demonstrated that the fluorescence response of the N,S-CDs with Cu2+ is highly selective for AA over other studied species. The high selectivity of the N,S-CDs–Cu2+ for AA is due to the fact that AA has stronger redox ability than the other reducing substances studied, and Cu+ ion has faster binding process and higher thermodynamic affinity with “S” of thiol ligand existed on N,S-CDs surface than other transition-metal ions.

As a probe used for practical applications, its fluorescence stability toward ambient environments is demanded. Fig. S7 exhibits emission spectra of the N,S-CDs with pH value in the range of 2–12. The PL behavior of the N,S-CDs was pH independent in the range of pH 5 to 9. However, the fluorescence peak decreased and shifted when the pH was lower than 5 or higher than 9. Such a pH effect could be attributed to the change in surface charge owing to protonation–deprotonation [3]. In addition, the fluorescent intensity of N,S-CDs scarcely showed alteration under continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp for 90min (Fig. S8A). Fig. S8B shows the photoluminescence intensity of N,S-CDs decreased only slightly with increasing NaCl concentration up to 3M. Moreover, the N,S-CDs displayed stable fluorescence at room temperature even after 3 months of storage. All the results above indicated the prepared N,S-CDs excellent stability, and tolerance for photobleaching and high-salinity. Therefore, the N,S-CDs are stable for practical application.

To test the application value of the method, the standard addition method was used to determine AA in human serum and urine samples (Human serums were collected from healthy laboratory volunteers at the college hospital, and urine samples were collected from healthy humans in our laboratory). The samples were processed as follows: 2.00mL of fresh serum sample and 0.5mL of trichloroacetic acid were mixed thoroughly, then centrifuged at 6000r.p.m. for 20min. The normal fresh urine samples were centrifuged at 6000r.p.m for 20min. A quantity of AA was added to the pretreatment samples before fluorescent analysis. As shown in Table 2, the relative standard deviation of the results for AA were lower than 2.4%, and the recoveries of AA were found to be in the range of 97.0–104.0%, suggesting that the method is reliable and applicable.

We have developed a simple, fast, one-step method to synthesize highly luminescent N,S-doped carbon dots by treating ethylenediamine and sulfamic acid under microwave irradiation, which provides a new route for synthesis of highly fluorescent, surface-different CDs. The prepared N,S-CDs exhibit good dispersibility, strong photoluminescence, and outstanding photostability. The obtained N,S-CDs with Cu2+ show highly selective fluorescent recognition to AA with a detection limit of 18nM. And this probe has been used to detect AA in biological fluids with high reproducibility and accuracy. Compared with some semiconductor quantum dots/organic dye-based fluorescence methods, the proposed strategy is much more environmentally friendly. All these features demonstrate that the proposed probe is promising for practical application in biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology ln University of Henan Province (15IRTSTHN 003)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.03.035.
